# Meme template catalog — generated by meme-seeder
# Format: INI-style, one section per template
# Compatible with you-get-a-meme's configparser loader
#
# Fields:
#   name           — display name
#   description    — semantic description for embedding search
#   caption_pattern — instructions for LLM caption generation
#   box_labels     — comma-separated labels, one per text box (in order)
#   tags           — comma-separated keywords
#   box_count      — number of text boxes in the template image


[0-days-without-lenny-simpsons]
name = 0 days without (Lenny, Simpsons)
description = Expressing ironic resignation when a recurring failure, bad habit, or workplace dysfunction happens again immediately after it was supposed to stop. Used when someone or something resets a 'days since incident' counter almost instantly, highlighting chronic dysfunction, inability to learn, or predictable chaos.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (the incident counter): The type of failure, bad habit, or embarrassing situation being tracked — what keeps happening. Box 2 (days count): The number of days since the last occurrence, almost always '0' to emphasize how quickly it happened again.
box_labels = incident type, days since
tags = recurring failure, zero days, chronic dysfunction, can't stop, here we go again, workplace chaos, bad habit, relapse, inevitable, reset counter, irony, exhaustion, workplace, again, predictable
box_count = 2

[absolute-cinema]
name = Absolute Cinema
description = Experiencing or witnessing something so undeniably, transcendently perfect that it demands reverent acknowledgment. Used when a moment, decision, or piece of work is so exactly right — so inevitable and complete — that it rises above ordinary praise into something almost cinematic.
caption_pattern = Top box (the subject): The thing, moment, decision, or outcome being elevated — what is being declared absolute cinema. Bottom box (the reaction/declaration): The verdict or proclamation, often simply affirming the subject as perfect, iconic, or inevitable — the 'this is it' statement.
box_labels = the subject, the declaration
tags = perfection, iconic, reverence, chef's kiss, peak, goat, masterpiece, this is it, flawless, appreciation, awe, validation, top tier, undeniable
box_count = 2

[always-has-been]
name = Always Has Been
description = Revealing that something widely assumed to be new, surprising, or recently noticed has always been that way — met with casual, unbothered acknowledgment. Used when someone acts shocked at a situation that was obviously always true, capturing the exhausted or smug 'yeah, no kidding' energy of someone who already knew.
caption_pattern = First astronaut (shocked discoverer): The thing being 'newly' revealed or noticed — framed as a surprising realization or question about something the speaker thought was different. Second astronaut (the one pointing the gun): The blunt, deadpan confirmation that it has always been this way — delivered as obvious, inevitable truth to someone who should have known.
box_labels = shocking realization, always has been
tags = always been this way, no kidding, obvious, revelation, cope, denial, it was always like this, conspiracy, harsh truth, disillusionment, sarcasm, been that way, reality check, discovering obvious truth
box_count = 2

[anakin-padme-4-panel]
name = Anakin Padme 4 Panel
description = Expecting someone to acknowledge an obvious implication or follow-through on something they just said, only for them to play dumb or remain oblivious — leaving you to spell it out yourself. Captures the tension of hinting at something you both clearly know, but the other person won't say out loud.
caption_pattern = Panel 1 (Anakin's setup): The statement, promise, or situation that logically implies an obvious conclusion — what Anakin says or does that sets up the expectation. Panel 2 (Padme hopeful): The obvious implication or hopeful conclusion the listener expects to follow — 'that means X, right?' Panel 3 (Anakin silent/ominous): No response, or a response that confirms the dark/uncomfortable truth — Anakin's silence or non-answer that signals the bad news. The absence of denial IS the punchline.
box_labels = anakin's statement, padme's hopeful assumption, anakin's silence / confirmation
tags = denial, implication, obvious conclusion, playing dumb, uncomfortable truth, oblivious, reality check, expectation, awkward silence, cope
box_count = 3

[ancient-aliens]
name = Ancient Aliens
description = Attributing an unexplained, unlikely, or suspiciously convenient phenomenon to a single implausible cause with complete confidence. Used when someone leaps to a wild conclusion, ignores obvious explanations, or insists a mysterious force is responsible for something that probably has a mundane answer.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (the mystery or observation): State the unexplained thing, weird coincidence, or puzzling event being analyzed. Box 2 (the absurd explanation): Deliver the outrageous, conspiracy-brained, or wildly disproportionate conclusion — always framed as the obvious answer.
box_labels = the unexplained thing, aliens (the absurd explanation)
tags = conspiracy, pseudoscience, wild conclusion, overthinking, blame, unexplained, leap of logic, delusion, cope, irony, absurdist, confident wrongness, impossible explanation, mystery, denial of reality
box_count = 2

[batman-slapping-robin]
name = Batman Slapping Robin
description = Shutting down a reasonable or obvious idea in favor of something more stubborn, impulsive, or contrarian. Used when someone is actively rejecting sensible advice, suppressing a thought, or dismissing a valid concern — often capturing overconfidence, willful ignorance, or a snap refusal to engage.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (Robin / the thing being silenced): The reasonable suggestion, logical course of action, or nagging thought being rejected — what someone probably *should* do or consider. Box 2 (Batman / the one doing the slapping): The forceful dismissal, the contrarian stance, or the impulse that shuts down the reasonable option — often representing ego, denial, or a stubborn authority figure.
box_labels = reasonable idea being rejected, the dismissive response
tags = rejection, dismissal, shutting down, contrarian, denial, stubbornness, bad decision, overconfidence, willful ignorance, suppression, authority, no, interrupting, impulse, refusal
box_count = 2

[bell-curve]
name = Bell Curve
description = Mocking the extremes of a spectrum for sharing the same trait as the average middle, while the middle is ridiculed. Used when two opposites (the ignorant beginner and the over-educated expert) agree on something that the 'midwit' mainstream dismisses — capturing the irony that simplicity loops back around from complexity.
caption_pattern = Left box (low IQ / simple): The naive or instinctive position — often correct but for unsophisticated reasons. Center box (mid IQ / midwit): The 'enlightened' mainstream take that overcorrects or overcomplicates, presented as the butt of the joke. Right box (high IQ / galaxy-brain): The position that loops back to the simple answer, but arrived at through deep reasoning or irony.
box_labels = simple take, midwit cope, galaxy-brain take
tags = midwit, galaxy brain, bell curve, irony, contrarian, overcomplicated, loops back, spectrum, elitism, dunning-kruger, agreement, simplicity wins, cope, big brain
box_count = 3

[bernie-i-am-once-again-asking-for-your-support]
name = Bernie I Am Once Again Asking For Your Support
description = Repeatedly asking for the same thing despite being ignored, dismissed, or given nothing. Used when someone is stuck in a loop of making the same request — politely, persistently, and with increasing exasperation — because nothing has changed.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (the asker, top): The person or entity doing the asking — usually 'me' or a stand-in character who keeps making the same request. Box 2 (the ask, bottom): The specific thing being requested again — the recurring need, demand, or plea that keeps going unanswered.
box_labels = who is asking, what they keep asking for
tags = persistence, repetition, begging, ignored, recurring request, exhaustion, polite frustration, broken record, still waiting, asking again
box_count = 2

[bernie-sanders-once-again-asking]
name = Bernie Sanders Once Again Asking
description = Repeatedly and persistently asking for the same thing that keeps getting ignored, denied, or deprioritized. Used when someone has been patiently requesting something for a long time and still hasn't gotten it — conveying weary determination mixed with mild exasperation.
caption_pattern = Top box (the asker): Identify who is making the request — often 'me' or a role/person known for persistent asks. Bottom box (the request): State the specific thing being asked for, repeatedly and without result — framed as an ongoing, unmet need.
box_labels = the asker, what they keep asking for
tags = persistence, ignored, repeated request, unmet need, asking again, frustration, patience, relentless, still waiting, advocacy
box_count = 2

[bike-fall]
name = Bike Fall
description = Attempting to do two things at once and failing spectacularly — getting cocky or distracted mid-task causes everything to fall apart. Used when overconfidence, multitasking, or a momentary lapse leads to an obvious self-inflicted disaster.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (the person/agent): The one attempting the action — a person, system, or concept that is trying to juggle or manage something. Box 2 (the thing being mishandled): The task, responsibility, or object being fumbled — what they should be focused on. Box 3 (the consequence): The resulting failure, crash, or disaster caused by the distraction or overreach.
box_labels = person doing the thing, thing being dropped, resulting failure
tags = fail, self-sabotage, overconfidence, multitasking, distraction, cause and effect, hubris, clumsy, backfire, own fault, cope, skill issue
box_count = 3

[boardroom-meeting-suggestion]
name = Boardroom Meeting Suggestion
description = Someone proposes a reasonable idea in a group setting, gets shut down by authority, then the same authority turns around and suggests the exact same idea as if it were their own — met with applause. Used when credit is stolen, ideas are rejected then recycled, or when the person with power gets praise for what others already said.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (suggester): Who made the original suggestion — typically a lower-status person like 'me', 'intern', or a name. Box 2 (the idea): The actual suggestion or proposal being made. Box 3 (authority figure): The boss or decision-maker who dismisses it — their rejection is implied by the structure. Box 4 (recycled idea): The same or nearly identical idea, now coming from the authority figure and being celebrated.
box_labels = suggester, the idea, boss/authority, boss repeating the idea
tags = credit stolen, idea theft, hypocrisy, workplace, boss, ignored, double standard, power dynamics, validation, irony, meetings, corporate, dismissed, rewarded, same idea
box_count = 4

[buff-doge-vs-cheems]
name = Buff Doge vs. Cheems
description = Contrasting a weak, outdated, or inferior version of something against a stronger, improved, or superior version. Used when celebrating an upgrade, mocking how something used to be done versus how it's done now, or highlighting that one approach completely dominates another — often with pride or swagger.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (weak Doge label): Label for the inferior, outdated, or lesser thing — typically a word or short phrase naming it. Box 2 (weak Doge description): What the weak version does or represents — its failure, limitation, or outdated behavior. Box 3 (buff Doge label): Label for the superior, upgraded, or dominant thing — the winner of the comparison. Box 4 (buff Doge description): What the strong version does or represents — its power, improvement, or advantage over the weak version.
box_labels = weak version label, weak version behavior, strong version label, strong version behavior
tags = comparison, upgrade, before and after, superior, inferior, glow up, flex, improvement, weak vs strong, then vs now, skill issue, dominance, evolution, better alternative, cope
box_count = 4

[change-my-mind]
name = Change My Mind
description = Confidently defending an unpopular, controversial, or absurd opinion as if it's obvious common sense. Used when someone holds a strong take they know others disagree with but refuses to back down — often ironic, provocative, or genuinely contrarian.
caption_pattern = Left panel (the claim): The opinion, hot take, or assertion being defended — stated as fact, usually bold or controversial. Right panel (the challenge prompt): Always 'Change My Mind' — this is fixed and should not be altered.
box_labels = the claim, change my mind
tags = hot take, opinion, debate, contrarian, unpopular opinion, confidence, provocation, argument, bold claim, defend
box_count = 2

[clown-applying-makeup]
name = Clown Applying Makeup
description = Convincing yourself that something increasingly absurd, delusional, or self-destructive is actually a good idea, escalating through stages of rationalization. Used when someone is clearly in denial, hyping themselves up for a bad decision, or watching their own logic spiral into clown behavior step by step.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (initial reasonable premise): The starting belief or situation that seems normal or defensible. Box 2 (first rationalization): The first stretch in logic — still plausible but starting to bend. Box 3 (deeper delusion): A more absurd extension of the rationalization, clearly heading somewhere bad. Box 4 (full clown mode): The final unhinged conclusion or action — the punchline where the person has fully committed to the bit.
box_labels = the reasonable premise, first rationalization, deeper delusion, full clown mode
tags = self-delusion, rationalization, cope, denial, escalation, bad decisions, galaxy brain, hyping yourself up, spiral, own worst enemy, irony, self-awareness
box_count = 4

[disaster-girl]
name = Disaster Girl
description = Gleefully causing or witnessing chaos while everyone else panics. Used when someone is secretly (or openly) the cause of a disaster and feels smug or unbothered about it — chaos agent energy, watching something burn with quiet satisfaction.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (the disaster): The catastrophic situation, crisis, or chaos unfolding in the background — what everyone else is horrified by. Box 2 (the culprit/subject): The person, thing, or force that caused it or is delighting in it — often 'me' or a relatable entity wearing an unsettling grin.
box_labels = the disaster, smug culprit
tags = chaos, schadenfreude, sabotage, smug, arson, villain era, watching it burn, caused this, unbothered, disaster
box_count = 2

[distracted-boyfriend]
name = Distracted Boyfriend
description = Abandoning what you already have or what you're supposed to be committed to the moment something newer, shinier, or more exciting comes along. Captures temptation, disloyalty, distraction, and the universal human tendency to want what you don't have — especially when it's right in front of you.
caption_pattern = Left figure (girlfriend, being ignored): The thing you already have, are committed to, or should be paying attention to — often the responsible, loyal, or expected choice. Center figure (boyfriend, looking away): The person, system, or entity being distracted — represents the subject of the meme (you, your brain, your company, etc.). Right figure (other woman, the distraction): The new, tempting, or shiny alternative that's pulling attention away — often frivolous, irresponsible, or simply more exciting.
box_labels = ignored girlfriend, distracted boyfriend, tempting distraction
tags = distraction, temptation, betrayal, preference, shiny new thing, ignoring, loyalty, wanting what you don't have, upgrade, comparison, jealousy, side-eye, cheating, fomo, attention
box_count = 3

[drake-hotline-bling]
name = Drake Hotline Bling
description = Rejecting the conventional, expected, or 'correct' option in favor of something more relatable, lazy, clever, or personally preferred. Used to express a clear preference where the chosen alternative feels more honest than what you 'should' do — especially when the rejected option is objectively better on paper but less appealing in practice.
caption_pattern = Top panel (rejected): The thing being dismissed — usually the responsible, conventional, or expected choice that Drake turns away from with disdain. Bottom panel (approved): The preferred alternative — often the lazier, funnier, more honest, or surprisingly niche thing Drake points to approvingly. The two options should be in direct contrast, with the bottom option revealing a personal truth, quirk, or relatable shortcut.
box_labels = rejected option, approved option
tags = preference, rejection, comparison, choosing, priorities, relatable, hypocrisy, upgrade, cope, irony, decision, versus, nope, yes, would rather
box_count = 2

[epic-handshake]
name = Epic Handshake
description = Two opposing groups or ideas that unexpectedly agree on something, revealing a shared interest, vice, or value that cuts across their usual divide. Used when highlighting unlikely alliances, common ground between rivals, or the moment two different types of people both enthusiastically endorse the same thing.
caption_pattern = Left hand (first group or position): One party, faction, ideology, or type of person involved in the agreement. Right hand (second group or position): The opposing or contrasting party that would not normally agree with the first. Center overlap (the shared thing): The specific behavior, belief, product, or outcome that both sides are jointly embracing — the punchline of the meme.
box_labels = first group, second group, what they both agree on
tags = agreement, alliance, common ground, unlikely duo, unity, both sides, shared interest, rivals agree, crossover, solidarity, hypocrisy, irony, we're the same, handshake
box_count = 3

[expanding-brain]
name = Expanding Brain
description = Escalating from a simple or obvious solution to an increasingly absurd, overcomplicated, or galaxy-brained alternative — each step feeling smarter than the last, even when it clearly isn't. Used when someone wants to mock intellectual overreach, rationalizing bad decisions as enlightened thinking, or the ironic pride of choosing the most convoluted path possible.
caption_pattern = Panel 1 (small brain, bottom): The most basic, obvious, or normie take — the starting point everyone else accepts. Panel 2 (slightly expanded brain): A mildly clever or unconventional variation that feels like a step up. Panel 3 (bigger brain): An increasingly unhinged or overcomplicated idea that the poster treats as brilliant. Panel 4 (galaxy brain, top): The final, most absurd or transcendent conclusion — presented with maximum ironic confidence as the ultimate enlightened take.
box_labels = basic take, slightly smarter take, big brain take, galaxy brain take
tags = galaxy brain, escalation, overthinking, rationalization, big brain, cope, irony, intellectual overreach, bad logic, upgrade, contrarian, smartest person in room, tiers, progression, enlightenment
box_count = 4

[grus-plan]
name = Gru's Plan
description = Making a plan that sounds logical until the final step reveals you were the one undermining it all along. Used when someone realizes mid-execution that their own scheme loops back against them — capturing self-sabotage, hypocrisy, or a plan that requires doing the very thing you were trying to avoid.
caption_pattern = Panel 1 (step one): The initial sensible-sounding action or premise. Panel 2 (step two): The logical continuation that builds on step one. Panel 3 (step three — repeated image): The supposed outcome, which is actually identical to panel 4. Panel 4 (shocked reaction): The reveal that the outcome is the same bad thing you started with, or that you failed to account for your own role — this is where the irony or self-own lands.
box_labels = step one, step two, step three (the plan), step three (the betrayal)
tags = self-sabotage, irony, hypocrisy, planning, backfire, own goal, plot twist, cope, loop, unintended consequences, overthinking, big brain, schemes, realization
box_count = 4

[hide-the-pain-harold]
name = Hide the Pain Harold
description = Smiling through discomfort, pain, or dissatisfaction while pretending everything is fine. Used when someone is clearly suffering or unhappy but masking it with a forced positive attitude — at work, in relationships, or in life generally.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (the painful situation being endured): The thing causing distress, frustration, or unhappiness that the person is tolerating with a fake smile. Box 2 (the forced positive reaction): The hollow affirmation, cope, or masked acceptance being performed despite the misery — often ironic or self-deprecating.
box_labels = the painful reality, forced smile response
tags = cope, masking, fake smile, suffering in silence, forced positivity, dissatisfaction, endurance, pretending, irony, toxic positivity, burnout, resignation, relatability
box_count = 2

[i-bet-hes-thinking-about-other-women]
name = I Bet He's Thinking About Other Women
description = Someone is preoccupied with a completely unrelated or unexpected thought while they appear to be in a normal, mundane situation — especially when a partner or observer assumes jealousy or distraction but the actual inner monologue is something absurd, nerdy, or hyper-specific. Used to reveal the gap between what others think you're thinking about and what you're actually obsessing over.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (the observer's assumption): What the person nearby assumes the distracted figure is thinking about — usually something socially expected like another person or a conventional concern. Box 2 (the actual thought): The real, often absurd, niche, or unexpected thing actually occupying the distracted person's mind — the punchline that subverts the assumption.
box_labels = what they think he's thinking about, what he's actually thinking about
tags = distraction, intrusive thoughts, hyperfocus, assumption wrong, inner monologue, oblivious, nerd, obsession, couple, misread, irony, daydreaming, what i'm actually thinking, jealousy subverted
box_count = 2

[imagination-spongebob]
name = Imagination Spongebob
description = Imagining or presenting something in an exaggerated, grandiose, or idealized way — especially when the reality is mundane, simple, or self-serving. Used when someone is rebranding a basic idea as profound, overhyping a minor thing, or sarcastically framing something as bigger than it is.
caption_pattern = First box (the setup): The plain, literal, or humble description of the thing — what it actually is. Second box (the reimagined version): The inflated, poetic, grandiose, or delusional spin the speaker puts on it — the rainbow hands reveal.
box_labels = what it actually is, how i'm choosing to see it
tags = imagination, rebranding, cope, delusion, spin, overhyping, self-deception, perspective, grandiose, euphemism, big brain, rationalization, comparison, irony, sarcasm
box_count = 2

[is-this-a-pigeon]
name = Is This A Pigeon
description = Confidently misidentifying or mislabeling something, treating an unrelated or obviously different thing as if it belongs to a known category. Used when someone enthusiastically applies the wrong framework, mistakes cause for effect, or naively equates two things that are not the same — especially when the error reveals ignorance, wishful thinking, or motivated reasoning.
caption_pattern = Left figure (the naive observer): The person, system, or entity doing the misidentifying — usually a stand-in for a group, institution, or mindset. Right figure (the butterfly / misidentified thing): The thing being incorrectly labeled — what is actually present. Bottom text (the wrong label): The incorrect category or name being confidently applied to the butterfly — what the observer wrongly believes it to be.
box_labels = the observer, the thing, wrong label
tags = misidentification, wrong, naive, confusion, false equivalence, mistaken, oblivious, labeling, category error, wishful thinking, irony, ignorance, cope, misunderstanding, overconfidence
box_count = 3

[left-exit-12-off-ramp]
name = Left Exit 12 Off Ramp
description = Choosing to abandon the expected or responsible path at the last moment in favor of something more appealing, tempting, or self-serving. Captures the moment of deliberate swerving away from what you 'should' do toward what you actually want — often used to mock one's own priorities, avoidance behavior, or sudden change of plans.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (driver label): The person, system, or entity making the choice — label them to set up the scenario. Box 2 (ignored exit): The responsible, expected, or boring option being bypassed — what they should do or where they should be going. Box 3 (taken exit): The tempting, surprising, or self-indulgent alternative they swerve toward instead.
box_labels = who is choosing, ignored option, chosen option
tags = avoidance, temptation, distraction, priorities, preference, impulsive, procrastination, swerve, detour, irresponsible, self-sabotage, choosing wrong, last minute, redirect
box_count = 3

[marked-safe-from]
name = Marked Safe From
description = Celebrating survival or avoidance of something annoying, stressful, or harmful that affected others. Used when you narrowly escaped a bad situation — a difficult meeting, a social obligation, a trend, a drama — while others were not so lucky. Tone ranges from genuine relief to smug self-satisfaction.
caption_pattern = Top box (the thing survived/avoided): Name the specific annoyance, danger, trend, or ordeal that others had to endure. Bottom box (the person or group who was spared): Identify who escaped — usually 'me', a specific person, or a defined group — framed as the lucky survivor.
box_labels = the thing avoided, who was spared
tags = relief, survival, avoidance, lucky, escaped, missed out, spared, smug, dodge, not my problem, exemption, opt out, narrow escape, trend, drama
box_count = 2

[megamind-peeking]
name = Megamind peeking
description = Sneaking a peek at something you weren't supposed to notice, or catching yourself being nosy, curious, or voyeuristic about something just out of reach. Used when someone is obviously lurking, eavesdropping, or peeking in where they don't belong — often self-aware and playful about the intrusion.
caption_pattern = Top box (the scene or barrier): What is being peeked at or the context that should be keeping the peeker out — the thing on the other side of the wall, the conversation, the situation. Bottom box (the peeker's identity or motivation): Who is doing the peeking, or what shameless curiosity or ulterior motive is driving them to look.
box_labels = what's being peeked at, the nosy peeker
tags = curiosity, nosy, lurking, eavesdropping, peeking, voyeur, uninvited, self-aware, intrusion, spying, none of my business but, checking in, watching, creeping
box_count = 2

[mocking-spongebob]
name = Mocking Spongebob
description = Mockingly repeating something someone said by mimicking their words in a sarcastic, alternating-caps tone to ridicule or dismiss it. Used when someone says something stupid, hypocritical, or annoying and you want to echo it back to highlight how absurd it sounds.
caption_pattern = Top box (original statement): The thing being mocked — a claim, excuse, complaint, or rule someone stated seriously. Bottom box (mocking echo): The same or paraphrased statement repeated in a sarcastic, dismissive tone — conveying 'can you believe this person said this.'
box_labels = original statement, mocking echo
tags = mockery, sarcasm, repetition, dismissal, ridicule, hypocrisy, cope, cringe, eye-roll, parroting
box_count = 2

[monkey-puppet]
name = Monkey Puppet
description = Caught off guard by something awkward, uncomfortable, or incriminating — and trying to pretend you didn't notice. Used when someone is visibly affected but attempting (and failing) to play it cool, look away, or act innocent after hearing or seeing something that implicates them.
caption_pattern = Left panel (the awkward trigger): The thing that was said, noticed, or revealed — the uncomfortable truth, call-out, or situation that demands a reaction. Right panel (the avoidance): The person, group, or self trying to subtly look away, ignore it, or pretend it didn't happen.
box_labels = the awkward moment, trying to ignore it
tags = awkward, avoidance, caught, pretending, side-eye, uncomfortable, denial, call-out, nervous, guilty, looking away, reaction

[mother-ignoring-kid-drowning-in-a-pool]
name = Mother Ignoring Kid Drowning In A Pool
description = Ignoring something important or urgent in favor of something trivial, irrelevant, or self-serving. Used when someone (a person, company, institution, or system) is visibly neglecting a serious problem while being distracted by or prioritizing something absurd or lesser. Captures willful blindness, misplaced priorities, and performative indifference to obvious harm.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (the mother / distracted party): The entity doing the ignoring — a person, company, government, or system. Box 2 (the phone / distraction): The trivial, irrelevant, or self-serving thing they are focused on instead. Box 3 (the child / drowning thing): The serious problem, urgent need, or important thing being ignored. Box 4 (optional label or 'meanwhile' context): Additional context, a punchline, or a label clarifying the stakes of what's being neglected.
box_labels = the ignorer, what they care about, what they're ignoring, meanwhile / stakes
tags = neglect, willful ignorance, misplaced priorities, distraction, indifference, cope, negligence, hypocrisy, irresponsibility, missing the point, wrong focus, disaster ignored, self-absorbed, side-eye
box_count = 4

[one-does-not-simply]
name = One Does Not Simply
description = Expressing that something is far more difficult, complex, or fraught than it appears — especially when an outsider assumes it should be easy. Used when navigating bureaucracy, social norms, or any system that punishes naive attempts at simplicity.
caption_pattern = Top box (setup): The seemingly simple action or task being proposed — phrased as if someone naively suggested it should be trivial. Bottom box (punchline): The implicit or explicit 'One does not simply...' continuation — the reason why it is actually impossible, dangerous, or absurd.
box_labels = the naive assumption, why it's not that simple
tags = impossible, harder than it looks, bureaucracy, gatekeeping, complexity, naive, underestimation, obstacles, reality check, not that easy, oversimplification, difficulty, frustration, irony, expectations vs reality
box_count = 2

[oprah-you-get-a]
name = Oprah You Get A
description = Enthusiastically distributing something to everyone without exception — often used when one thing applies universally, when someone is being overly generous, or when a single solution is being applied to every problem indiscriminately. Captures the energy of mass giveaways, blanket policies, or viral spread where everyone gets the same thing whether they want it or not.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (Oprah's announcement): The thing being given out to everyone — a reward, a punishment, a trend, a feeling, or a consequence. Box 2 (the gift): The specific item, outcome, or thing that every single person is receiving — often ironic, absurd, or disproportionate in scale.
box_labels = announcement, the thing everyone gets
tags = generosity, universal, everyone, giveaway, indiscriminate, mass distribution, you get a, viral, abundance, irony, blanket policy, same for everyone, enthusiasm, reward, punishment
box_count = 2

[pawn-stars-best-i-can-do]
name = Pawn Stars Best I Can Do
description = When someone offers you something but it's far less than what you asked for, need, or deserve — and they present it as the best possible deal despite falling woefully short. Captures the frustration of lowball offers, half-measures, and situations where the other party refuses to budge even when their offer is clearly inadequate.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (what you want/need): The full ask — the reasonable request, desired outcome, or fair value being sought. Box 2 (the lowball counter): The insufficient offer being presented as a take-it-or-leave-it deal, typically a fraction of what was requested.
box_labels = what you want, best i can do
tags = negotiation, lowball, underpaid, undervalued, rejection, compromise, unfair offer, take it or leave it, frustration, cheap, dealmaking, insufficient, cope, skill issue
box_count = 2

[roll-safe-think-about-it]
name = Roll Safe Think About It
description = Feeling smug or self-satisfied about a technically clever but obviously flawed or self-serving plan. Used when someone convinces themselves that a lazy, dishonest, or delusional solution is actually genius — especially when the 'big brain' logic only works if you don't think too hard about it.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (setup — the problem or situation): The circumstance or pressure that motivates the person to scheme, often a responsibility, cost, or consequence they want to avoid. Box 2 (the 'genius' move): The flawed, self-serving, or circular logic they use to sidestep it — presented with unearned confidence.
box_labels = the problem, the 'big brain' solution
tags = self-deception, big brain, galaxy brain, smug, loophole, cope, logic, clever, delusional, avoidance, rationalization, irony, overconfidence, lazy solution, flawed reasoning
box_count = 2

[running-away-balloon]
name = Running Away Balloon
description = Avoiding or running away from one thing while being obliviously pulled toward another, more dangerous or ironic thing. Used when someone escapes one problem only to walk straight into a worse one, or when a person, idea, or system is fleeing something minor while ignoring a much bigger threat right in front of them.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (the person/entity running): Who or what is trying to escape something. Box 2 (the thing being fled): The problem, fear, or responsibility being avoided — usually something manageable or minor. Box 3 (the balloon string the runner is holding): The thing the runner thinks is safe, helpful, or neutral — their chosen escape route or coping mechanism. Box 4 (the balloon itself): The ironic twist — the 'safe' thing turns out to be dangerous, counterproductive, or the real threat. Box 5 (optional label or punchline): A caption that names the irony, identifies the runner, or delivers the final twist.
box_labels = the runner, thing being avoided, perceived solution, actual danger, punchline / label
tags = irony, avoidance, out of the frying pan, blissful ignorance, cope, self-sabotage, unintended consequences, escapism, denial, jumping from bad to worse, oblivious, running away, problem solving gone wrong, upgrade fail
box_count = 5

[sad-pablo-escobar]
name = Sad Pablo Escobar
description = Waiting alone with nothing to do, nobody around, and time passing with no stimulation or interaction. Captures the feeling of being stranded, ignored, forgotten, or left with no options — sitting in awkward silence, refreshing your phone, or killing time when plans fall through.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (who/what is absent): The person, thing, or event that was supposed to show up but didn't — the source of the abandonment. Box 2 (the waiting figure): Who is doing the waiting, usually 'me' or a relatable subject. Box 3 (the void they're sitting in): The empty, boring, or hopeless context — what's around them while they suffer through the wait.
box_labels = who/what didn't show up, the one left waiting, the empty situation
tags = waiting, abandoned, loneliness, boredom, ignored, ghosted, left out, nothing to do, no one came, forgotten, stuck, isolation, disappointment, plans fell through, time passing
box_count = 3

[soldier-protecting-sleeping-child]
name = Soldier protecting sleeping child
description = Fiercely protecting something valuable, trivial, or embarrassing while neglecting what actually matters. Used when someone guards a lesser priority with disproportionate energy — often with irony or self-awareness about misplaced effort.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (the soldier / protector): The thing doing the protecting — often the person, habit, or force standing guard. Box 2 (the sleeping child / protected thing): The thing being shielded — usually trivial, embarrassing, or ironic given what's being sacrificed for it. Box 3 (the threat being held off): The thing being kept away — often something reasonable, important, or obviously correct that is being ignored or suppressed.
box_labels = the protector, what's being protected, what's being blocked
tags = protection, priorities, misplaced effort, irony, sacrifice, self-sabotage, denial, guarding, cope, avoidance, twisted loyalty, bad priorities
box_count = 3

[squidward-window]
name = Squidward window
description = Secretly watching or waiting for something you pretend not to care about — the quiet anticipation of someone lurking from a distance, hoping something will happen without admitting they want it. Used when you're low-key invested, envious, or waiting for drama to unfold while maintaining a facade of indifference.
caption_pattern = Top panel (the watcher's context): Who Squidward is or what role the observer is playing — the person pretending not to care or waiting in secret. Bottom panel (the thing being watched): What they're actually fixated on — the event, person, or outcome they're desperately hoping for despite pretending otherwise.
box_labels = the watcher, what they're watching for
tags = lurking, anticipation, pretending not to care, jealousy, secret interest, waiting, hope, envy, low-key obsessed, side-eye
box_count = 2

[theyre-the-same-picture]
name = They're The Same Picture
description = Pointing out that two things someone treats as distinct are actually identical, usually to expose hypocrisy, false choice, or a distinction without a difference. Used when someone insists two options are different but they lead to the same outcome, or when two things that sound different are functionally equivalent.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (Pam, the presenter): The label or category of the thing being compared — what the person is presenting as two distinct items. Box 2 (photo A): The first option or thing, framed as different from the second. Box 3 (photo B): The second option or thing — which is actually the same as the first.
box_labels = presenter label, thing A, thing B
tags = same thing, false dichotomy, hypocrisy, no difference, equivalence, distinction without a difference, two options, irony, exposure, they're the same
box_count = 3

[this-is-fine]
name = This Is Fine
description = Calmly accepting or ignoring a situation that is clearly spiraling out of control, pretending everything is fine while chaos unfolds around you. Captures forced normalcy under obvious disaster — denial, burnout, or choosing not to panic when panic would be the rational response.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (the dog/character): The person, role, or entity that is choosing to ignore the surrounding chaos — often 'me' or a specific person/system. Box 2 (the environment on fire): The actual situation that is bad, worsening, or out of control — the disaster being normalized.
box_labels = the person coping, the disaster being ignored
tags = denial, cope, burnout, chaos, normalcy, disaster, ignoring problems, this is fine, calm in crisis, avoidance, overwhelmed, pretending, it's fine, catastrophe, detachment
box_count = 2

[this-is-where-id-put-my-trophy-if-i-had-one]
name = This Is Where I'd Put My Trophy If I Had One
description = Sarcastically highlighting the absence of something you never had or were never given a chance to earn. Used when someone points out a gap, lack, or missing achievement with bitter humor — implying the system, situation, or lack of opportunity is the real problem, not personal failure.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (location/shelf label): The physical or conceptual place where the thing would go — usually 'This is where I'd put my [X] if I had one.' Box 2 (the missing thing): The specific reward, credential, achievement, or resource that is conspicuously absent.
box_labels = place for it, the thing i don't have
tags = absence, sarcasm, lack, no reward, missed opportunity, cope, bitter, gatekeeping, never got a chance, hypothetical
box_count = 2

[trade-offer]
name = Trade Offer
description = Framing a lopsided or absurd exchange as a formal negotiation where one side gives up something valuable and receives something questionable in return — and presenting it straight-faced. Used when calling out unfair deals, bad tradeoffs, or hilariously unequal arrangements, especially in workplace, relationship, or social dynamics.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (your offer label): A short label identifying the person or party making the offer, typically 'I receive' or the offeror's name. Box 2 (what you give up): The thing being sacrificed or given — usually something genuinely valuable, like time, money, dignity, or effort. Box 3 (what you get back): The absurd, insulting, or laughably small return — what the other party is offering in exchange.
box_labels = offeror label, what you give, what you receive
tags = negotiation, unfair deal, bad trade, exploitation, workplace, comparison, irony, imbalance, sacrifice, cope
box_count = 3

[tuxedo-winnie-the-pooh]
name = Tuxedo Winnie The Pooh
description = Contrasting a crude or basic version of something with a refined, sophisticated, or elevated version of the same thing — used when someone upgrades their thinking, taste, or phrasing while keeping the core intent identical. Captures the feeling of performing class or intelligence while doing something that is fundamentally the same as the lowbrow version.
caption_pattern = Bottom panel (lowbrow version): The plain, crude, or unsophisticated way of stating or doing something — the version people might be embarrassed to admit. Top panel (elevated version): The same thing reframed in fancy, intellectual, or sophisticated language — dressed up but functionally identical to the bottom panel.
box_labels = lowbrow version, elevated version
tags = upgrade, sophistication, same thing, fancy vs basic, reframing, pretentious, self-aware, irony, class, relatable
box_count = 2

[two-buttons]
name = Two Buttons
description = Feeling torn between two options that both feel necessary or appealing, leading to paralysis or a stressed refusal to choose. Used when someone is caught between two competing impulses, obligations, or temptations and can't bring themselves to pick one — especially when both choices feel urgent or equally bad/good.
caption_pattern = Button 1 (left option): The first competing choice, obligation, or temptation — something the person feels compelled to press. Button 2 (right option): The second competing choice — equally compelling, contradictory, or mutually exclusive with the first. Sweating figure (the person): The entity, person, or system caught between the two options, unable to decide.
box_labels = left button, right button, person struggling
tags = indecision, dilemma, impossible choice, paralysis, torn, both options, conflict, competing priorities, stress, choosing between
box_count = 3

[uno-draw-25-cards]
name = UNO Draw 25 Cards
description = Refusing to take the easy or obvious solution because it requires one uncomfortable step, then choosing to endure a much worse alternative instead. Captures the irrational avoidance of a small obligation when the cost of avoiding it is clearly higher.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (the avoided option): The simpler or correct action that requires something mildly annoying, embarrassing, or effortful. Box 2 (the absurd alternative): The much worse thing the person chooses instead — framed as 'drawing 25 cards' — to avoid doing Box 1.
box_labels = avoided option, drawing 25 cards
tags = avoidance, denial, stubbornness, irrational, cope, commitment issues, choosing worse, refusal, self-sabotage, procrastination
box_count = 2

[waiting-skeleton]
name = Waiting Skeleton
description = Waiting indefinitely for something that never comes, or has taken absurdly longer than expected. Used when enduring an endless delay with resigned patience — a deadline that keeps slipping, a person who never follows through, or a process that seems to take forever.
caption_pattern = Top box (the thing being waited for): What was promised, expected, or long overdue — the thing the skeleton has been waiting on. Bottom box (the elapsed time or context): How long the wait has been, or the situation that has stretched on indefinitely.
box_labels = thing being waited for, how long / context
tags = waiting, patience, delay, procrastination, never happens, overdue, broken promise, indefinite, forever, long time, still waiting, unreliable, time passing, exhaustion, disappointment
box_count = 2

[woman-yelling-at-cat]
name = Woman Yelling At Cat
description = When someone calmly presents a perfectly reasonable take and gets met with total emotional overreaction or irrational rejection. Used when one side is being dramatic, delusional, or unreasonable while the other remains unfazed — captures the absurdity of arguing with someone who won't accept reality or common sense.
caption_pattern = Left panel (the yelling woman): The irrational, dramatic, or emotional party — states their unreasonable position, accusation, or demand. Right panel (the unbothered cat): The calm, unbothered, or factually correct party — delivers the deadpan rebuttal, reality check, or simply exists as the thing being blamed.
box_labels = yelling woman, unbothered cat
tags = overreaction, drama, irrational, calm, denial, accusation, reality check, hypocrisy, deadpan, side-eye, cope, unbothered, argument, gaslighting, delusion
box_count = 2

[x-x-everywhere]
name = X, X Everywhere
description = Noticing the same thing appearing everywhere once you become aware of it, or sarcastically pointing out that something is absurdly ubiquitous or overrepresented. Used when a pattern, person, problem, or phenomenon seems to dominate every context — at work, at home, online, in the news.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (the thing that's everywhere): Name the recurring element — a person, concept, problem, buzzword, or phenomenon that keeps appearing everywhere. Box 2 (the observation): Mirror or echo Box 1 with '[X], [X] everywhere' to complete the Buzz Lightyear phrasing, or simply repeat the subject to emphasize its omnipresence.
box_labels = the thing, it's everywhere
tags = ubiquity, everywhere, pattern recognition, oversaturation, repetition, irony, overused, noticing, sarcasm, inescapable, trending, buzzword, it's everywhere
box_count = 2

[yall-got-any-more-of-that]
name = Y'all Got Any More Of That
description = Craving or repeatedly returning to something enjoyable, pleasurable, or satisfying — especially after already having it once or running out. Used when expressing insatiable appetite, addictive desire, or needing more of a good thing, often with self-aware humor about the compulsion.
caption_pattern = Top box (the thing desired): Name the object, experience, substance, or situation being craved — whatever you can't get enough of. Bottom box (the asker, optional context): Usually left as the implicit speaker or used to specify who is doing the craving or what triggered the need for more.
box_labels = thing being craved, context or who's asking
tags = craving, addiction, wanting more, insatiable, obsession, running out, can't stop, need more, desire, relapse
box_count = 2

[you-guys-are-getting-paid]
name = You Guys are Getting Paid
description = Discovering that others are being compensated or rewarded for something you've been doing for free, out of loyalty, or naively assuming was unpaid for everyone. Used when expressing disbelief that you were the only one not getting paid, credited, or recognized while everyone else was.
caption_pattern = Box 1 (the naive assumption): The thing you were doing without compensation or expectation of reward — the unpaid labor, free service, or assumed-volunteer situation. Box 2 (the shocking reality): The revelation that others involved were actually getting paid, rewarded, or compensated the whole time.
box_labels = what you've been doing for free, what everyone else was getting
tags = disbelief, exploitation, naive, unpaid, betrayal, labor, compensation, surprise, sucker, working for free, inequality, taken advantage of, volunteer, underpaid
box_count = 2
